title: SEGMENT 14 — Vertex VX-880: islet cells for type 1 diabetes

lines.text: SEGMENT 14 — Vertex VX-880: islet cells for type 1 diabetes

I want to spend some time on the diabetes story, because it illustrates both the promise and the complications of iPSC-style cell therapy beautifully, even though the most prominent clinical program here uses ES-derived rather than iPSC-derived cells.
Type 1 diabetes is, fundamentally, a problem of missing beta cells.
The patient's immune system has destroyed the insulin-producing cells in the pancreas, and now the patient cannot make insulin.
They have to inject it, multiple times a day, for the rest of their lives.
Insulin therapy is life-saving but imperfect — patients still have huge fluctuations in blood glucose, devastating long-term complications, and the constant management burden.
The obvious therapeutic dream, for decades, has been: just give the patient new beta cells.
Transplant healthy islets into their body and let them make their own insulin again.
Pancreatic islet transplants from deceased donors have been done for a long time and they work — temporarily, for some patients.
But cadaver islets are scarce, the quality varies, and the procedure is complex.
The supply is fundamentally limited.
What if you could make beta cells in unlimited quantity from a stem cell line?
You could treat millions of patients.
The supply problem disappears.

A biotech company called Semma Therapeutics, founded by a Harvard researcher named Doug Melton, spent over a decade working out how to differentiate stem cells into functional, glucose-responsive beta cells.
Melton had two children with type 1 diabetes and the work was personal.
Semma was acquired by Vertex Pharmaceuticals in 2019, and Vertex took the program into clinical trials.
The drug is called VX-880.
Here's how VX-880 works.

They take human embryonic stem cells — though similar work is being done with iPSCs — and differentiate them through the long multi-step protocol I described earlier, ending up with islet-like cell clusters that contain beta cells, alpha cells, and the other islet cell types, all functional in vitro.
They infuse those cells into the patient's portal vein, the vein that carries blood to the liver.
The cells take up residence in the liver, where they sense blood glucose and release insulin in response.
The patient gets immunosuppression to prevent rejection — because these are ES-derived, not autologous.
The early results, reported in 2023 and 2024, were genuinely stunning.
Patients on VX-880 saw their daily insulin requirements drop dramatically.
Some came off insulin entirely.
The first patient enrolled in the trial, a man named Brian Shelton, achieved insulin independence for the first time in decades.
He has, by 2026, remained off insulin for several years.
He died unrelated to the treatment a few years in, but during his life, his diabetes was — functionally — cured.
Now, VX-880 uses ES-derived cells, not iPSC-derived ones, and the patients require immunosuppression.

The next-generation effort, also from Vertex, is VX-264 — encapsulated beta cells that don't require immunosuppression because they're protected inside a permeable device that lets insulin and glucose pass through but blocks immune cells.
And further out, there are programs working on truly iPSC-based versions where the cells come from off-the-shelf allogeneic banks or — eventually — from the patient's own reprogrammed cells.
The reason I tell you this story is twofold.

First, it's a real example of cell-replacement therapy actually working in patients — and that wouldn't have been imaginable without the differentiation protocols developed for iPSCs and ES cells together.
The Yamanaka-era pluripotency revolution provided the foundation.
Second, it shows you the trajectory the field is on.
The first wave of clinical successes is using ES-derived cells with immunosuppression.
The second wave, coming now, will use encapsulation or HLA-matched allogeneic iPSCs to reduce or eliminate immune issues.
The third wave, eventually, will deliver patient-specific or universal-donor cells, off-the-shelf, for all kinds of cell loss diseases.
